items.q: What attitude does Pop Art take toward consumer culture?
items.correct: Ambiguous - it both celebrates and critiques mass media imagery.
items.why: Students read Pop Art as either pure celebration or pure protest. Warhol's deadpan repetition of Marilyn or soup cans is deliberately AMBIGUOUS, leaving viewers to decide if it's homage or critique.

items.q: What did Renaissance artists rediscover that medieval art lacked?
items.correct: Linear perspective and naturalistic representation of the human body.
items.why: Students assume medieval art was colourless. It was vivid but flat - the Renaissance breakthrough was mathematical PERSPECTIVE (Brunelleschi) and anatomical realism, not the introduction of colour.

items.q: Why is understanding patronage important for studying historical art?
items.correct: Patrons commissioned art for specific purposes, shaping content and form.
items.misconception: Artists worked freely - patronage was irrelevant to the artwork.
items.why: Students romanticise the lone artist. Most pre-modern art was COMMISSIONED, with patrons specifying subject, materials, even composition. Understanding the commission illuminates choices the artist made.
The answer examiners want is: Van Gogh. “Monet” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Linseed oil. “Water” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Approximate century when oil painting became dominant in Europe?
items.why: Exact answer: 15 century. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
